summary: I was born just eight months after the Second World War ended, in February 1946, in Oakland, California. My father served in the United States Navy during the war and made five major landings in the South Pacific before he turned 18. He was a radar man and experienced severe shell shock, what we now call PTSD, from his wartime service.

I was born in Oakland, California, at the Kaiser hospital in 1946. I grew up in West Oakland, which was the largest per capita African American community west of the Mississippi at the time. My early schooling was in predominantly Black elementary schools in Oakland, and later I attended junior high there before moving to South Berkeley.
cues: largest African American community west of the Mississippi

summary: My maternal grandfather’s father jumped ship in the port of Boston and eventually found work in textile factories in Paterson, New Jersey. This was part of my family’s immigrant story, with roots stretching back to the early 20th century. Boston was a key location in that migration path.

Paterson, New Jersey, was where my great-grandfather worked in textile factories after arriving from Boston. This was part of my mother’s family history, reflecting the working-class immigrant experience in the early 1900s. It connects to my family’s roots in the industrial labor movement.

My maternal grandmother was born to a Unitarian Norwegian family and taught school at the Rosebud Indian Reservation in Dakota when she was just 17 or 18 years old. She and her sister worked there as young teachers, which was quite an unusual and formative experience for them. The Rosebud Reservation was a significant place in my family’s history.

My grandfather, Henry Eugene McGuckin, was a member of the Industrial Workers of the World, or IWW, in the 1910s. He was part of the Flying Squad, riding the rails to wherever there was labor unrest or free speech movements, organizing workers and giving soapbox speeches. His activism was centered in places like Butte, Montana, and he lived the hobo life from a very young age.

Butte, Montana, was one of the main places where my grandfather was active as a member of the Industrial Workers of the World. He was involved in labor organizing and free speech movements there during the 1910s. Butte was a hub for his work riding the rails and supporting workers’ rights.

The Memoirs of a Wobbly is a book that contains my grandfather’s recollections as a member of the Industrial Workers of the World. It was published by Kerr Press, which I believe is based in Chicago. My uncle was responsible for publishing it, preserving my grandfather’s stories from his time on the Flying Squad.

Kerr Press, located in Chicago, published The Memoirs of a Wobbly, which is based on my grandfather’s experiences with the Industrial Workers of the World. My uncle was involved in getting this book published, helping to preserve our family’s labor history. Kerr Press played a key role in sharing those stories.

My mother worked in a warehouse and was part of Warehouse Local 6 shortly after I was born. Later, my stepfather, Roscoe Quincy Proctor, also worked in the warehouse sector and was a member of Local 6 in the East Bay. This union was important in our family’s working-class life and political activism.

My biological father, William Joseph Maher, served in the United States Navy during the Second World War. He was a radar man in the South Pacific and participated in five major landings before he was 18. His service left him with severe shell shock, which affected his life deeply.

The South Pacific was where my father served during the Second World War as a radar man in the Navy. He took part in five major landings there before turning 18, which was a harrowing experience. The trauma he endured in the South Pacific had lasting effects on his life.

William Joseph Maher was my biological father, who served in the Navy during the Second World War. He was shell shocked from his service in the South Pacific and left my mother when I was very young. I didn’t meet him until I was 44 years old, and I took my stepfather’s name instead when I was 16.

My mother, Virginia Adair McGuckin, was born in 1926 on a mountaintop outside Eugene, Oregon. She was of Irish and Norwegian descent and joined the Communist Party when she was 14. She worked in a warehouse and was a single mother for a time before marrying my stepfather.

My mother was born on a mountaintop outside Eugene, Oregon, in 1926. Her birth was paid for in dried fruit and apricots, reflecting the rural and modest circumstances of her family. Eugene was the starting point of her journey before moving west to California.

My mother joined the Communist Party USA when she was just 14 years old. My stepfather, Roscoe Quincy Proctor, was also deeply involved in the Party and was considered a diehard Black communist. Their political beliefs shaped much of my upbringing and family life.

My mother and stepfather met while working at Colgate-Palmolive, both employed through Warehouse Local 6. This was shortly after the war, and it was part of their working-class lives in the Bay Area. Their union work and political activism were intertwined with their jobs there.

My stepfather, Roscoe Quincy Proctor, worked as a casual on the docks in Los Angeles during or just after the war. He heard about organizing drives in the Bay Area and moved north to join the warehouse sector there. Los Angeles was an important early chapter in his labor career.

The East Bay was where my stepfather, Roscoe Proctor, worked in the warehouse sector and was a member of Local 6. It was also where I grew up, particularly in West Oakland and later South Berkeley. The East Bay was central to our family’s labor and political life.

Roscoe Quincy Proctor was my stepfather, an African American from a sharecropper family in east Texas. He worked on the docks in Los Angeles before moving to the East Bay to work in warehouses and join Local 6. He was a committed communist and was indicted under the Smith Act for refusing to register as an agent of a foreign power.

The Smith Act was the law under which my stepfather, Roscoe Proctor, was indicted. He was ordered to register as an agent of a foreign power because of his ties to the Communist Party USA, facing fines and prison if he refused. His trial took place in the Old San Francisco Mint, but he was never jailed.

The Old San Francisco Mint was the site of my stepfather Roscoe Proctor’s trial under the Smith Act. The trial was presided over by a former Alabama governor who was a Black man, which was notable. This place holds significance in our family’s political history.

Alabama figures into my story because the judge at my stepfather’s Smith Act trial was a former governor of Alabama. This was an interesting detail given the racial and political dynamics of the time. It added a layer of complexity to the trial held in San Francisco.

Fred Thompson was a man tried alongside my stepfather, Roscoe Proctor, under the Smith Act. While Roscoe was tried in San Francisco, Fred was tried on the East Coast. Their trials were part of the government’s crackdown on Communist Party members during that era.

Henry Winston was the chairman of the Communist Party USA and was also charged under the Smith Act like my stepfather. Unlike many others charged, he was not foreign-born, which was unusual for those targeted. His leadership role made him a significant figure in the Party during that time.

The FBI was involved in monitoring and harassing my family from a young age. I remember being approached by FBI agents when I was just a kid in Oakland, with men in cars trying to get me to ride with them or talk. Their presence was a constant reminder of the political scrutiny we lived under.

I Led Three Lives was a show or reference connected to the FBI’s anti-communist activities during my childhood. I recall people from that program’s context trying to talk to me or my family. It symbolized the kind of government pressure and suspicion we faced growing up.

I attended Hoover Junior High School in Oakland for the first six weeks of seventh grade. It was a predominantly Black school with a small percentage of white and Latino students. This was before my family moved to South Berkeley, where I attended a more integrated school.

After leaving West Oakland, my family moved to South Berkeley, where I attended Willard Junior High School. The neighborhood was working class but well integrated, with Black, white, and Japanese American families living nearby. Many of the Japanese families had been interned during the war, which was a significant part of the community’s history.

Willard Junior High School in South Berkeley was where I went after moving from Oakland. It was a well-integrated school with students from diverse racial backgrounds. I had a memorable experience trying to make friends there, including riding my bike to visit a white classmate’s home.

In South Berkeley, many of the Japanese American families living near us had been interned during the war. Their presence and history were a visible part of the neighborhood’s fabric. This experience shaped my awareness of racial injustice from a young age.
